skill.name: k8s-security-policies
skill.description: Design Kubernetes security policies for network isolation, pod hardening, RBAC, admission control, and service mesh security.
skill.summary: Create practical Kubernetes security policies for network, pod, RBAC, and admission-control hardening.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged network endpoints, command execution patterns, weak crypto terms, and reconnaissance words. Manual review found these are Kubernetes YAML examples, Markdown code fences, kubectl troubleshooting commands, and metadata-service blocking rules, not executable malicious behavior. The skill is safe to publish with a low-risk note that permissive sample policies must be reviewed before production use.

low_findings.title: Permissive Kubernetes Examples Require Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for privileged pod-security labels, wildcard RBAC permissions, secret access, and broad HTTPS egress. These examples are presented as policy templates or troubleshooting material, not as hidden execution paths, but users should adapt them before production use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly contain permissive Kubernetes policy examples. They are documentation content, so the risk is operational misuse rather than malicious code.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Matches Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution patterns. Manual review shows these are Markdown fenced YAML and bash examples, including kubectl troubleshooting commands, with no script that executes them automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is visible in Markdown documentation blocks. No executable wrapper, installer, or runtime hook is present in the reviewed files.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes teams need practical policies that reduce network, pod, and access-control risk. This skill provides templates and guidance for NetworkPolicy, Pod Security Standards, RBAC, OPA Gatekeeper, and Istio controls.
actual_capabilities: Provides NetworkPolicy examples for default deny, DNS, ingress, monitoring, HTTPS egress, databases, and cross-namespace traffic.
Explains namespace labels for privileged, baseline, and restricted Pod Security Standards.
Shows RBAC Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ServiceAccount, and least-privilege access patterns.
Includes OPA Gatekeeper label enforcement examples for admission control.
Covers Istio PeerAuthentication and AuthorizationPolicy examples for service mesh security.
Offers troubleshooting commands for NetworkPolicy and RBAC permission issues.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, not live cluster scanning or enforcement.
Examples require review against the target Kubernetes version, CNI plugin, and namespace labels.
Some examples are intentionally broad to explain patterns and should be narrowed for production.
It does not replace a full Kubernetes security assessment or compliance audit.

use_cases.title: Secure a New Namespace
use_cases.description: Create default-deny networking, allow DNS, and apply restricted pod security labels before deploying workloads.

use_cases.title: Review Application Access
use_cases.description: Design service accounts and RBAC roles that grant only the resources and verbs an application needs.

use_cases.title: Prepare Compliance Controls
use_cases.description: Map Kubernetes controls to common security expectations for segmentation, admission control, logging, and least privilege.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Namespace Baseline
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new namespace
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Kubernetes security baseline for a new production namespace. Include pod security labels, default-deny NetworkPolicy, DNS egress, and key review notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build App Network Policies
prompt_templates.prompt: Design NetworkPolicy rules for a frontend, backend, database, monitoring, and ingress controller in one namespace. Explain each allowed path.

prompt_templates.title: Design Least-Privilege RBAC
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a least-privilege RBAC plan for a Kubernetes application service account that reads one ConfigMap and updates one Deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Multi-Tenant Cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Review a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster security model. Cover Pod Security Standards, RBAC boundaries, NetworkPolicy isolation, admission control, and Istio authorization.

output_examples.input: I need to secure a production namespace before deploying services.
output: A namespace hardening checklist with restricted pod security labels.
A default-deny network policy approach with specific DNS and ingress allowances.
Review notes for CNI support, rollout order, and application-specific exceptions.

output_examples.input: Our app service account needs access to configuration and deployments.
output: A least-privilege RBAC design that separates read and update permissions.
Recommendations to avoid wildcard resources and verbs.
A validation plan using kubectl auth can-i checks.

output_examples.input: We need admission control for required labels.
output: A Gatekeeper policy approach for required labels on selected workload types.
Guidance on audit mode, enforcement rollout, and exception handling.
Operational checks for teams before enforcement is enabled.

best_practices: Start with audit or staging mode before enforcing policies in production namespaces.
Prefer explicit resources, verbs, service accounts, ports, and selectors over wildcard rules.
Validate generated policies with cluster-specific CNI, admission controller, and workload requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not copy privileged namespace labels into production without a documented exception.
Do not grant cluster-wide secret access when a namespace Role or named resource is enough.
Do not allow broad egress without blocking metadata services and documenting required destinations.

faq.question: Does this skill apply policies to my cluster?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates and guidance. You must review and apply changes with your normal deployment process.

faq.question: Can it help with PodSecurityPolicy?
faq.answer: It discusses pod security concepts, but modern clusters should use Pod Security Standards because PodSecurityPolicy is removed from current Kubernetes releases.

faq.question: Does it cover NetworkPolicy for all CNI plugins?
faq.answer: It gives standard NetworkPolicy examples. You must confirm that your CNI plugin supports the required behavior.

faq.question: Can it generate least-privilege RBAC?
faq.answer: Yes, it can guide Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, and ServiceAccount design based on requested resources and verbs.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: The skill content is safe to publish. Production policies still require environment-specific review and testing.

faq.question: Does it include compliance mapping?
faq.answer: It references CIS Kubernetes Benchmark and NIST concepts, but it does not perform a formal compliance audit.
